g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore two fundamental long-term behaviors of Markov chains: stationary distributions and absorbing states.
What happens to a Markov chain after many transitions? Does it settle into a predictable pattern or get trapped somewhere?
g.text: What happens in the long run? Does the system settle into a predictable pattern
or get trapped in certain states?

g.data-narration: We study two scenarios: regular chains reach equilibrium (stationary distribution), while absorbing chains get trapped permanently.
A stationary distribution π is a probability vector where π multiplied by P equals π, representing equilibrium.
g.text: A probability vector π = [π₁, π₂, ..., πₙ] is stationary if:

g.data-narration: To find π: write π × P = π, simplify to homogeneous equations, replace one with normalization, then solve.
g.text: How to Find the Stationary Distribution
Step 1: Write π × P = π as linear equations
Step 2: Rearrange to π × (P - I) = 0 (homogeneous system)
Step 3: Replace one equation with π₁ + π₂ + ... + πₙ = 1

g.data-narration: For regular chains: unique stationary distribution exists, all states visited with positive probability, and P^k converges to a steady matrix.
g.text: Fundamental Theorem for Regular Markov Chains
If P is a regular stochastic matrix, then:

g.data-narration: Example: For matrix with p11=0.7, solving π×P=π gives π=[4/7, 3/7] - the system spends 57% in state A.
For a 3-state chain, solving the system gives π=[2/5, 1/5, 2/5] - states 1 and 3 have equal long-run probability.
An absorbing state i has p_ii = 1, meaning once entered, the chain can never leave it.
g.text: A state i is absorbing if p_ii = 1
This means: once the chain enters state i, it can never leave. All other transitions from i are zero.

g.data-narration: An absorbing Markov chain has at least one absorbing state, and from every non-absorbing state, reaching an absorbing state is possible.
g.text: A Markov chain is absorbing if:
1. At least one absorbing state exists
2. Every transient state can reach an absorbing state

g.data-narration: The canonical form arranges absorbing states first: P = [I O; R Q], where I is identity, O is zero, R and Q are submatrices.
g.text: With r absorbing states and t transient states (n = r + t):

g.data-narration: The fundamental matrix N = (I-Q)^(-1) = I + Q + Q² + Q³ + ... gives expected visits to each transient state before absorption.

text.tspan: N = (I - Q)⁻¹ = I + Q + Q² + Q³ + ...

g.text: where I is t×t identity matrix, Q is transient-to-transient submatrix.
N_ij = Expected visits to transient state j

g.data-narration: Expected absorption time from state i equals row sum of N. Absorption probability matrix B = N × R gives probability of ending in each absorbing state.
g.text: t_i = Σⱼ N_ij (sum of row i in N)
B = N × R gives probability of absorption
into each absorbing state from each transient state

g.data-narration: For example: with Q=[[0.5,0.25],[0,0.5]], N=[[2,1],[0,2]], absorption takes 3 steps from state 1 and 2 from state 2.
Regular chains converge to stationary distribution; absorbing chains eventually get trapped. Tools differ: solving π×P=π versus computing N=(I-Q)⁻¹.
Key formulas: π×P=π, p_ii=1 for absorbing, N=(I-Q)⁻¹, t_i = sum(N_ij), B = N×R. Master these for exams!
Key formulas to remember: stationary distribution satisfies π×P=π, absorbing states have p_ii=1, fundamental matrix N=(I-Q)⁻¹.
